summary: Gruber shares observations from WWDC 2008, covering iPhone 3G upgrade considerations, App Store pricing strategy, the influx of new developers to the Apple ecosystem, and impressive WebKit advances. He notes that jailbreak interest has evaporated now that official APIs exist, and that developers are enthusiastic about Cocoa Touch after last year's disappointing web-only approach. He speculates about iPod Touch pricing pressure from the $199 iPhone 3G and predicts Apple will respond with price cuts and new software rather than hardware changes.
key_points: AT&T's limited 3G coverage means many iPhone upgraders will still be stuck on EDGE despite paying higher monthly rates
App Store pricing will skew toward free and $9.99, but Apple will carefully balance free vs. paid to encourage impulse buys over mere impulse downloads
WWDC attracted a significant wave of new-to-Cocoa developers for both iPhone and Mac, reflecting a broadening developer ecosystem
WebKit advances (canvas, SVG, advanced CSS) are powerful enough to build slick web apps targeting only modern browsers, and someone who does this will break out
The $199 iPhone 3G creates awkward pricing pressure on the $299 iPod Touch, which Gruber expects Apple to address with price cuts and new software in September
money_quotes: Things look good if you live in or near a major metropolitan area; if you don't, well, I hope you like EDGE.
Developers, by and large, are very happy with Apple's Cocoa Touch APIs and developer tools — a far cry from last year's "sweet" shit sandwich.
Apple wants to encourage impulse buys, not merely impulse downloads.
The combined market share for, say, Firefox 3 and Safari 3 is larger than the overall market share for Mac OS X.

Plenty of developers write desktop software that only works on the Mac — why aren't more people writing web apps that only work in truly modern web browsers? The first one to do it is going to be a sensation.
If sales drop because potential iPod Touch buyers opt for a $199 iPhone 3G instead, don't expect many tears from Apple executives.
key_insight: WWDC 2008 marked a turning point where Apple's developer ecosystem rapidly expanded beyond its traditional base, with official iPhone APIs killing jailbreak interest and WebKit pushing the boundaries of what modern browsers could do.
